claims.claim_text: The Change Healthcare breach involved ALPHV/BlackCat using compromised credentials to access a remote portal without MFA, followed by lateral movement and data exfiltration.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: This is a real incident with specific attacker, access vector, MFA failure, and timeline details documented in official testimony, but the primary source host is off the shared allowlist.

claims.claim_text: UnitedHealth estimated first-quarter cyberattack effects of $872 million.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The dollar figure is specific and real, but the most direct primary source is an SEC filing on an off-allowlist domain.

claims.claim_text: Google's BeyondCorp effort began around 2011 and was documented in a series of papers published from 2014 through 2018 in USENIX ;login:.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: The Assume-Breach Posture on Bare Metal
claims.rationale: The claim is real and verifiable from Google's publication index, but the primary host is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Google later published BeyondProd to extend zero-trust principles to workload and service identity in cloud-native environments.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: The Assume-Breach Posture on Bare Metal
claims.rationale: The official BeyondProd paper directly describes Google extending zero-trust concepts from user/device access to workload identity and service-to-service trust.

claims.claim_text: Without NetworkPolicy, pods are allowed to communicate freely, and an empty podSelector default-deny policy selects all pods in the namespace.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: The Assume-Breach Posture on Bare Metal
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes NetworkPolicy documentation directly covers default allow behavior and the effect of an empty podSelector.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy operates at L3/L4 and cannot express L7 HTTP method, path, or header policy.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: The Assume-Breach Posture on Bare Metal
claims.rationale: This is standard Kubernetes behavior documented in the upstream NetworkPolicy reference and examples.

claims.claim_text: John Kindervag coined the term 'Zero Trust' in a 2010 Forrester report titled 'No More Chewy Centers: Introducing the Zero Trust Model of Information Security'.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Government Standards and Maturity Models
claims.rationale: The historical attribution is real, but the primary source is a Forrester page outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Executive Order 14028 was signed on May 12, 2021 and directed federal agencies to plan for zero trust architecture.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Government Standards and Maturity Models
claims.rationale: NIST's EO 14028 guidance page explicitly gives the date and states that Section 3 directs agencies to plan to implement zero trust architecture.

claims.claim_text: OMB Memorandum M-22-09 was issued on January 26, 2022 and required agencies to meet specific zero-trust objectives by the end of FY2024.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Government Standards and Maturity Models
claims.rationale: This memorandum is the primary source for the date and deadline, but the authoritative host is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: NIST SP 800-207 was finalized on August 11, 2020 and defines the Policy Engine, Policy Administrator, and Policy Enforcement Point logical components.
claims.span_hint: section: NIST SP 800-207
claims.rationale: The shared pool NIST SP 800-207 source directly covers the publication date and the PE/PA/PEP model.

claims.claim_text: NIST SP 800-207 defines seven tenets of zero trust architecture.
claims.span_hint: section: NIST SP 800-207
claims.rationale: The shared pool NIST SP 800-207 publication is the direct source for the canonical ZTA tenets.

claims.claim_text: NIST SP 800-207A was published on September 13, 2023 and addresses cloud-native applications, service mesh, and SPIFFE-related access control patterns.
claims.span_hint: section: NIST SP 800-207
claims.rationale: The NIST SP 800-207A publication page directly states the publication date and cloud-native, service-mesh, and SPIFFE scope.

claims.claim_text: CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model v2.0 was published on April 11, 2023 and organizes zero trust around five pillars, three cross-cutting capabilities, and four maturity stages.
claims.span_hint: section: CISA and DoD Maturity Models
claims.rationale: CISA's official ZTMM page directly supports the publication date and the model's pillars, cross-cutting capabilities, and stages.

claims.claim_text: The DoD Zero Trust Reference Architecture Version 2.0 was published in September 2022, and the DoD Zero Trust Strategy defines 91 target-level capability outcomes and 61 advanced-level outcomes.
claims.span_hint: section: CISA and DoD Maturity Models
claims.rationale: These are specific DoD document facts, but the authoritative DoD CIO source is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module asserts an April 12, 2026 status statement about the not-yet-confirmed publication of DoD Zero Trust Strategy 2.0.
claims.span_hint: section: CISA and DoD Maturity Models
claims.anchor_text: As of April 12, 2026, while the community anticipates the DoD Zero Trust Strategy 2.0 and early 2026 timelines were discussed by DefenseScoop in late 2025, no authoritative source confirms the document was published, and the expected release remains unverified.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Check the DoD CIO site for the current publication status of the next Zero Trust Strategy update.
claims.rationale: This is a time-sensitive editorial status claim tied to an off-allowlist trade press report and should be rewritten to avoid freezing an unverifiable current-state assertion into the module.

claims.claim_text: SPIFFE and SPIRE reached CNCF Graduated status in August 2022, with SPIRE on August 22 and SPIFFE on August 23.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Workload Identity (SPIFFE/SPIRE)
claims.rationale: The CNCF project pages directly provide the exact graduation dates for SPIRE and SPIFFE.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the latest stable SPIRE release is v1.14.1 from January 15, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Workload Identity (SPIFFE/SPIRE)
claims.anchor_text: The latest stable release of SPIRE (SPIFFE Runtime Environment) is v1.14.1, released January 15, 2026.
claims.suggested_rewrite: SPIRE continues to publish stable releases; check the project's release page for the current version.
claims.rationale: The claim is outdated because later stable SPIRE releases exist, so the sentence should be rewritten to avoid a stale 'latest version' assertion.

claims.claim_text: An SVID can be encoded as either an X.509 certificate or a JWT token, and the SPIFFE Workload API is exposed locally over a Unix domain socket.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Workload Identity (SPIFFE/SPIRE)
claims.rationale: These are core SPIFFE facts, but the canonical SPIFFE documentation host is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Cilium became a CNCF Graduated project on October 11, 2023.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Network Microsegmentation
claims.rationale: The CNCF graduation announcement directly supports the date.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the current stable Cilium release is v1.19.2 and that v1.20.0 is pre-release.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Network Microsegmentation
claims.anchor_text: The current stable Cilium release is v1.19.2, released March 23, 2026; v1.20.0 is in pre-release.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cilium maintains a current v1.19 stable branch, and newer v1.20 builds may be in pre-release; check the release page for the latest patch level.
claims.rationale: The 'current stable' patch-level assertion is already stale because newer stable Cilium releases exist.

claims.claim_text: Cilium v1.19 introduced strict encryption enforcement modes that drop unencrypted traffic in strict mode instead of relying on best-effort behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Network Microsegmentation
claims.rationale: The operational detail is documented in Cilium's own docs, but that documentation host is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Linkerd was the first service mesh to achieve CNCF Graduated status on July 28, 2021.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: mTLS Everywhere and Service Mesh
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page directly provides the graduation date.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the current stable Linkerd release is 2.18 released on May 9, 2025.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: mTLS Everywhere and Service Mesh
claims.anchor_text: The current stable Linkerd release is 2.18, released May 9, 2025.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Linkerd continues to publish stable releases; check the releases page for the current version.
claims.rationale: The statement is stale because Linkerd 2.19 exists, and the cited 2.18 date is also not the current stable reference point.

claims.claim_text: Istio became a CNCF Graduated project on July 12, 2023.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: mTLS Everywhere and Service Mesh
claims.rationale: The CNCF graduation announcement directly supports the date and project status.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the current latest stable Istio release is v1.29.0 and ties it to the Kubernetes 1.31-1.35 support matrix.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: mTLS Everywhere and Service Mesh
claims.anchor_text: The current latest stable Istio release is v1.29.0, released February 16, 2026, supporting Kubernetes 1.31–1.35.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Istio 1.29 is a supported release line; check the supported-releases page for the latest patch version and Kubernetes compatibility matrix.
claims.rationale: The release-line compatibility is still useful, but the 'latest stable is 1.29.0' assertion is already stale because newer 1.29 patch releases exist.

claims.claim_text: Istio ambient mode reached General Availability with the v1.24 release in November 2024.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: mTLS Everywhere and Service Mesh
claims.rationale: Istio's official 1.24 release announcement directly states that ambient mode became generally available in that release.

claims.claim_text: Under strict mTLS, Istio rewrites HTTP/TCP/gRPC probes by default so kubelet health checks go through the sidecar rather than directly to the application.
claims.span_hint: section: The Kubelet Health Check Problem
claims.rationale: Istio's health-check documentation explicitly explains probe rewrite behavior and states that HTTP, TCP, and gRPC probes are rewritten by default.

claims.claim_text: Open Policy Agent became a CNCF Graduated project on January 29, 2021.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Continuous Verification and Admission Control
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page directly lists the graduation date.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the latest OPA Gatekeeper release is v3.22.0 published in March 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Theory: Continuous Verification and Admission Control
claims.anchor_text: OPA Gatekeeper (the Kubernetes ValidatingAdmissionWebhook for OPA) latest release is v3.22.0, published March 2026.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OPA Gatekeeper continues to publish current releases; check the project's release page for the latest version.
claims.rationale: The 'latest release' claim is already stale because later Gatekeeper releases exist.

further_reading.title: NIST SP 800-207: Zero Trust Architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the foundational zero-trust architecture document the module repeatedly references.

further_reading.title: NIST SP 800-207A: Cloud-Native Access Control Model
further_reading.why_relevant: This NIST follow-on publication is the most directly relevant standard for Kubernetes, SPIFFE, and service-mesh zero-trust design.

further_reading.title: Istio Security Concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: It provides the upstream conceptual basis for mTLS, authorization policy, identity, and enforcement behavior used throughout the lab.

notes: Repeated facts in the 'Did You Know?' section were not duplicated as separate seed entries when they restate earlier citation points. Quiz answers and code blocks were excluded per the module-seeding rules.
